error.signature: AI suggests a wall clock or watch as a gift for a Chinese business partner or elder
error.regex: (china.*clock.*gift|gift.*china.*watch|送钟|送終|送终|chinese.*clock.*present|gift.*chinese.*new year|chinese gift taboo)

verdict.summary: Giving a clock (送钟 sòng zhōng) is a near-homophone for 送終 (sòng zhōng, 'attending a funeral' / 'paying last respects'). Giving a wall clock, mantel clock, or ornamental time-piece to a Chinese recipient — especially an elder or business partner — is widely read as wishing them death. Wristwatches (手表) are generally fine; the taboo is specific to 钟.

dead_ends.action: Give a branded corporate wall clock or desk clock as a business gift to a Chinese counterpart
dead_ends.why_fails: The phrase 送钟 is almost indistinguishable from 送終 (attending a dying relative). Older Chinese recipients in particular take strong offense; even younger counterparts will feel the host culturally tone-deaf. The clock is typically not kept.

dead_ends.action: Bundle a clock with other items to 'dilute' the meaning
dead_ends.why_fails: The symbolism of the clock is dominant; bundling does not neutralize it. Recipients remember the clock.

workarounds.action: Substitute a wristwatch (手表) — no homophonic taboo. For corporate gifts, quality tea (high-grade longjing, pu'er), fine liquor (premium baijiu), or pastries in even-numbered counts (but avoid 4) work well.

Also avoid: green hats (implies cuckoldry, 戴绿帽), white/black wrapping (funeral colors), handkerchiefs (farewell implication), shoes (suggests the recipient will walk away), and pears (分梨 sounds like 分离, separation).
